summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Where the wife seeks injunctive orders for the preservation of property – Where the husband disposed of a luxury motor vehicle and remitted the proceeds of sale overseas – Where the wife contends the husband was the legal and beneficial owner of the motor vehicle – Where the husband contends the motor vehicle was owned by a company and the sale proceeds were remitted in payment of a liability of the company to a third party within the ordinary course of business – Where the husband had previously disclosed in court documents under oath that he was the legal owner of the motor vehicle – Where the husband failed to provide financial records of the company proving its ownership of the motor vehicle – Where it is not established that the company had a pre-existing liability owed to the third party at least in the amount of the proceeds of sale of the motor vehicle – Where it is not established that the proceeds of sale are not within the husband’s possession or control – Order made directing the husband to cause the proceeds of sale of the motor vehicle to be paid into the wife’s solicitor’s trust account pending further order.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAMPTON J:

These reasons determine the relief as sought in paragraphs 1, 2 and 6 of an Application in a Proceeding filed on 17 April 2026 for injunctive orders for the preservation of property brought by Ms Khuu (“the wife”) being $500,000 received on or around 15 April 2026 on the disposal of Motor Vehicle 1 registration “[...]” in the context of complex proceedings for property adjustment pursuant to s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The orders sought are:
Pending further order, the Respondent Husband, [Mr Trang] whether by himself, his servants, agents, employees or otherwise howsoever, be restrained from removing from Australia or in any way disposing of, dealing with, encumbering, charging, transferring, dissipating, diminishing the value of, or otherwise parting with possession or control of any of his assets up to the unencumbered value of $500,000.00, including without limitation:
(a) the proceeds of sale of [Motor Vehicle 1] registration [...], sold on or about 15 April 2026 for the sum of $500,000.00 ("[Motor Vehicle 1] Proceeds");

(b) any funds in any bank or financial-institution account in his sole or joint name, or under his ownership or control, into which [Motor Vehicle 1] Proceeds (or any part thereof) have been paid, deposited, transferred or traced; and

(c) any chattels, instruments, securities or other property purchased with, or substituted for, any part of [Motor Vehicle 1] Proceeds.

Within 24 hours of service of these Orders, the Respondent Husband transfer the sum of $500,000.00 (representing [Motor Vehicle 1] Proceeds), or such part thereof as remains in his possession, ownership or control, into the trust account of Luminous Legal, to be held in that trust account pending further order of the Court or written agreement between the parties.
...
The Respondent Husband pay the Applicant Wife's costs of and incidental to this Application, on an indemnity basis pursuant to section 114UB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).

(As per the original)
Mr Trang (“the husband”) by way of a Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 21 April 2026 seeks:
That the applicant’s application in a proceeding filed on 17.04.2026 be dismissed
2[.] A declaration that [Motor Vehicle 1] was trading stock of [B2 Pty Ltd] and its disposal was a transaction in the ordinary course of business
A finding that the application of the sale proceeds to satisfy the liability of [Mr C] does not constitute a dissipation of the matrimonial asset pool.
That the Applicant pay the First Respondent’s costs of and incidental to this application.
Notwithstanding the summary nature of the hearing today, each of the husband and the wife urges the Court to make findings of fact.
The wife seeks findings of fact that:
(a) On or about 15 April 2026, the husband was the legal and beneficial owner of Motor Vehicle 1; and

(b) The husband has received the $500,000 by way of the proceeds of the sale and has retained those monies in his possession or control.

The husband seeks findings of fact that:
(a) In lieu of paragraph 2 in his Response to an Application in a Proceeding by way of declaration, that Motor Vehicle 1 was trading stock of B Pty Ltd and its disposal was a transaction in the ordinary course of business of that corporation; and

(b) The application of proceeds of sale have been remitted to China to satisfy a liability of the corporation payable to Mr C.

BACKGROUND

The parties commenced cohabitation in early 2012. They married in 2016 and separated on 25 March 2024. There are no children of the marriage.
On 16 September 2024, the wife commenced proceedings in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) seeking orders for property adjustment between she and the husband. On 23 November 2024, the husband filed a Response to an Initiating Application seeking different orders as to the adjustment of property.
On 28 November 2024, an order was made as follows:
Restraints
That unless otherwise agreed between the parties in writing and without admissions each of the Husband and the Wife (both in their individual capacity, and in their capacity as Directors/Shareholders or officeholders in any business) be hereby restrained from:
Disposing, encumbering or further encumbering or increasing the indebtedness secured against any real properties in their sole or joint names, or held by any entity in which they have an interest;
Disposing or encumbering any share or interest they hold in a private company;
Opening any new bank accounts in their names, or in the name of any business in which they hold an interest;
Withdrawing, transferring, or otherwise dealing with funds in any bank accounts that are under his or her name (either jointly or solely) and control, except for the following:
i. Their reasonable living expenses; and

ii. Legal costs in relation to these Proceedings.
Withdrawing, transferring, or otherwise dealing with funds in any bank accounts that in the name of a company or entity in which they hold an interest, except for the following expenditure in the ordinary course of business.
Disposing or encumbering of any item of personal property;
Loaning or borrowing any funds from a third party without the wife’s written consent.
On 4 March 2025, the proceeding was transferred to this Court.
The balance sheet filed by the wife on 1 August 2025 records her contentions that the value of the property of the parties available for division is in the range of $6,000,000.
The husband confirmed in the hearing before me today his contentions that the liabilities of the parties have a higher value than their interests in property. He describes it as a “negative pool”.
On 4 February 2026, the wife observed that Motor Vehicle 1 was listed for sale by D Motor Group for $759,990. Her solicitor wrote to the husband. She contended that a disposal of the vehicle would contravene Order 6(f) made on 28 November 2024 in circumstances where she asserted at that time the vehicle was registered in the husband’s name. The husband replied to the wife’s solicitor that the vehicle had not been sold and that he would withdraw it from sale. In email exchanges between the wife’s solicitor and the husband, the husband advised by email to the wife’s solicitor on 5 February 2026:
I confirm that I will comply with the court orders made on 28 November 2024. Specifically, Order 6, Disposing or encumbering of any item of personal property.
(As per the original)
On 10 April 2026, the wife served the husband with an Amended Initiating Application and affidavit. The interlocutory relief sought by the wife in that document put the husband on notice that she sought to have Motor Vehicle 1 transferred to her by way of interim property settlement and proposed to sell the motor vehicle and retain the proceeds of sale.
On 17 April 2026, the husband disclosed to the wife by way of a financial statement that Motor Vehicle 1 had been sold on 15 April 2026 for $500,000. The wife thereafter initiated the filing of her Application in a Proceeding on that day.
The husband’s evidence is that his corporation, B Pty Ltd, obtained a New South Wales (“NSW”) motor vehicle dealer licence in 2019. On 21 September 2021, that corporation purchased Motor Vehicle 1 from D Motor Group for $759,999. He adduced into evidence an invoice from D Motor Group Pty Ltd dated 21 September 2021 confirming those assertions.
It is the husband’s evidence that the vehicle was “at all times held by the company as trading stock for the purpose of resale in the ordinary course of the [c]ompany’s business”. He asserts that the vehicle was part of the “corporate inventory” at the time of the orders made on 28 November 2024. He did not tender the statutory financial statements of the corporation as at 30 June 2025, being a balance sheet and possibly depreciation schedule, so as to give evidence to ground his assertion.
The husband says that B Pty Ltd has been the subject of an Australian Taxation Office (“ATO”) audit for a number of years, and that in March 2025, after receiving advice from an unknown person, he became aware of a potential fringe benefits tax liability relating to Motor Vehicle 1. It is his evidence that on 9 April 2025 he attended Services NSW in Suburb E to “update the registration details” of Motor Vehicle 1, being what he describes as an “administrative action” to minimise the corporation’s taxation liabilities. He asserts that this conduct did not change the “character of the vehicle as trading stock”. The wife directly puts these contentions into issue.

THE LEGAL AND BENEFICIAL OWNER OF MOTOR VEHICLE 1 REGISTRATION “[...]” ON 15 APRIL 2026

Exhibit 2 is the Transport for NSW Certificate of Registration for Motor Vehicle 1 expiring on 20 December 2025. That document records the husband as the person holding the Certificate of Registration. The husband identifies that the certificate itself records “the registered operator of the vehicle” and that “it is not proof of vehicle ownership”.
Exhibit 2 records that a transfer of ownership fee of the vehicle was paid and a transfer effected on 9 April 2025, that stamp duty was paid on that transfer of $11,600, and that a plate content transfer fee of $242 was paid.
The wife today has carefully constructed a forensic exploration of a series of documents that either originate from the husband’s solicitors, implicitly on his instructions, or is evidence the husband has sworn to be accurate in these proceedings to date. As part of that process, the wife has adduced letters prepared by solicitors, F Lawyers, who at the relevant times identified later in these reasons have acted for both the husband personally in these proceedings and also for B Pty Ltd in the ATO audit process.
Exhibit 3 is paragraph 28 of an affidavit of the husband filed in these proceedings on 31 July 2025. The evidence of the husband on oath records:
On or around 9 April 2025, I purchased [Motor Vehicle 1] with plate number [...] from [B Pty Ltd]...
(Emphasis removed)
Exhibit 4 is a Financial Statement of the husband prepared by his then solicitors sworn on 25 July 2025 and filed on the same day. The Financial Statement records the following affidavit:
(a) I have read section 71B or 90RI of the Family Law Act and Rule 6.06 of the Family Law Rules and I am aware that by law I have an obligation to make a full and frank disclosure of my financial circumstances to the Court and each other party. In particular, I have disclosed in this document or in an affidavit filed by me or on my behalf under Rule 6.06(5), all matters I am required to disclose under the Family Law Act and the Family Law Rules.

(b) The information in the financial statement and any attachments to it which are within my personal knowledge are true. Where I have given an estimate in this financial statement, it is based on my knowledge and is given in good faith. All other information given in this financial statement and any attachments is true to the best of my knowledge, information and belief

(c) I have no income, property or financial resources other than as set out in this document or any affidavit filed by me under Rule 6.06(5).

(As per the original)
At item 40 in Exhibit 4, the husband gives evidence as to he being the legal and beneficial owner of Motor Vehicle 1, asserting an estimated value of $700,000. He records a separate interest in Exhibit 4 by way of his 100 per cent shareholding in B Pty Ltd.
Exhibit 5 is an undertaking as to disclosure filed on 25 July 2025, executed by the husband and prepared by his solicitors. That undertaking is certified by his then lawyer. It records as follows:
I have read, or had read to me, sections 71 B or 90RI of the Family Law Act 1975 (for financial or property matters) and Parts 6.1 and 6.2 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021;
I am aware of my duty to the Court and lo each other party including any independent children's lawyer to give full and frank disclosure of all information relevant to the issues in the case in a timely manner;
I undertake to the Court that to the best of my knowledge and ability, I have carried out and complied with my duty of disclosure;
I undertake to continue to comply with my duty of disclosure until the conclusion of the case;
I understand the nature and terms of this undertaking and that if I breach the undertaking I may be guilty of contempt of court.
(As per the original)
Each of Exhibits 3, 4 and 5 were prepared by F Lawyers as the husband’s solicitors at the same time as F Lawyers was acting for B Pty Ltd in the ATO audit.
Exhibit 1 includes at page 66 a document entitled “Wholesale Tax Invoice for Used Vehicle”. It records a tax invoice number and is dated 15 April 2026. The document records that B Pty Ltd issued the invoice to G Pty Ltd, recording an address of the recipient at N Street Suburb J and an email address for “[Mr K]”. The invoice contains what appears to be a motor vehicle dealer licence number for B Pty Ltd and a motor vehicle dealer licence number for G Pty Ltd.
The invoice identifies Motor Vehicle 1 and a selling price of $500,000 inclusive of GST at $45,454.55. The invoice directs the payment of funds to a L Bank account number ending...06 in the name of B Pty Ltd.
Absent further evidence, the invoice concludes that the vehicle has been disposed of to another motor dealer.
The findings sought by each party as to whether the husband was the legal and beneficial owner of Motor Vehicle 1 must be established on the balance of probabilities. All evidence must be weighed and assessed having regard to the capacities of the parties to adduce and contradict it (Vetter v Lake Macquarie City Council (2001) 202 CLR 439).
The wife had little if any capacity to adduce into evidence the published financial statements of B Pty Ltd for the year ending 30 June 2025. It would be expected on the husband’s case that they would include Motor Vehicle 1 as an asset of that entity at that time. The wife had little if any capacity to produce probative evidence as to the insurance for the motor vehicle subsequent to the transactions at Services NSW on 9 April 2025.
The husband had the capacity to put into evidence the bank account of B Pty Ltd with the L Bank account number ending...06, recording the receipt of funds on the disposal of the vehicle. He has not done so.
The husband has failed to explain why stamp duty was payable on the transfer transaction at Services NSW on 9 April 2025. The wife identifies that sections of the Duties Act 1997 (NSW) do not require stamp duty to be paid on the trading stock of motor vehicle dealers.
A letter from the husband’s solicitors to the ATO in the audit process, contained in Exhibit 1, dated 29 August 2024, records that the corporation at that time had one vehicle and that any private use of any other vehicle is accounted for through a “FBT employee contribution”. It further records that the corporation does not hold stock nor has any ownership of stock, its business activity involving collecting funds from customers and transferring funds to external platforms and returning those funds to customers. It is against that background that the wife contends that the implication from the husband’s evidence that B Pty Ltd engages in the buying and selling of motor vehicles as a dealer is a ruse.
A consideration of all of the above evidence directs a finding that as at 15 April 2026 the husband was the legal and beneficial owner of Motor Vehicle 1 registration “[...]”.
This conclusion is directed by way of the payment of stamp duty on the Transport for NSW Certificate of Registration document on 9 April 2025 (Exhibit 2), the husband not explaining why stamp duty was payable, the husbands’ representations on oath as contained in Exhibit 3 and Exhibit 4 on 25 July 2025, being 3 months after the date of that transfer, supplemented by the contents of the husband’s undertaking as to disclosure also provided on 25 July 2025 in Exhibit 5, as recorded earlier herein.
The finding is buttressed by his then solicitors, F Lawyers, also acting for him in the ATO audit proceedings. The solicitors implicitly were aware as to a requirement not only to be accurate in the representations made by their client to this Court, but also to the Commonwealth revenue authority.

PROCEEDS OF SALE

The husband’s evidence is that he caused the $500,000 received on the disposal of Motor Vehicle 1 on 15 April 2026 to be “applied in their entirety to discharge a pre-exi[s]ting business liability” of B Pty Ltd payable to Mr C, a resident of China, and that the monies were remitted to that individual in China. He contends that the remission of funds had a “net neutral impact on the balance sheet by exchanging a physical asset for an equivalent reduction in debt”.
The import of the husband’s affidavit evidence is that $500,000 received on the disposal of Motor Vehicle 1 was paid into a B Pty Ltd corporate bank account with L Bank ending in...06. There is no explanation as to why the husband has not disclosed the bank account statement or adduced it into evidence. Implicitly that bank statement would record the receipt of the funds of $500,000 and then the transfer of those funds out of the account on 16 April 2026 to a currency exchange entity, M Financial Services, converting those funds to Chinese currency.
He has not adduced documentary evidence recording the remission of those funds from Australia to China. He has had the opportunity to disclose with precision the identity of the bank account from which the funds were transferred from this country, the identity of the account to which the funds were paid, and the dates of each transaction. He did not do so.
I find the husband is aware of his duty to the Court and the other party to give a full and frank disclosure of all information relevant to the issues listed for hearing today. He has on no less than two occasions recorded in documents filed in this court confirmed, with the benefit of advice from his solicitors, that he has read or has had read to him s 71B of the Act and ch 6 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”).
The construction of s 71B of the Act directs the disclosure of information by way of explanation of relevant financial circumstances. Longstanding authority establishes that in the circumstances of disclosure failures as to documents and information relevant to financial circumstances of a party, the court may take a more robust approach in making findings by way of inference in favour of the innocent party, especially in circumstances for the purposes of urgent injunctive relief.
Here, the husband has the documents and information available to him to easily cure the evidentiary deficits identified in these reasons. The urgent listing of the matter has not permitted the wife to engage with those deficiencies by way of the issuing of a subpoena, or by way of other forensic processes.
On a consideration of the above matters, I am not satisfied that the husband has established to the requisite degree that the proceeds of sale of Motor Vehicle 1 are no longer in his possession or control. I find that the said proceeds of sale of the motor vehicle, as at the date of these reasons, are in his possession or control, such that he has a capacity to comply with an order that he cause them to be deposited in a bank account should the wife achieve the injunctive relief she seeks for the preservation of funds.

THE DEBT TO MR C

The husband’s affidavit filed on 21 April 2026 gives evidence as to B Pty Ltd lending funds to other entities for profit and borrowing funds from private lenders. As part of this evidence, he asserts that between 1 July 2025 and 20 April 2026, the corporation had received no additional funds but had discharged $1,204,464 to private lenders including at least $500,000 to Mr C. Implicitly the $500,000 to Mr C is linked to the sale of Motor Vehicle 1.
The husband placed into evidence a document in the Mandarin language with an artificial intelligence translation which purported to be an agreement in writing between B2 Pty Ltd (not B Pty Ltd) and Mr C. The document records that since 2017, Mr C has provided “multiple commercial loans” to B2 Pty Ltd. The document confirms that as at 7 November 2024 the cumulative principal due to Mr C was AUD $919,391 and that any advances by way of loan were “interest-free”.
The wife contends that the document may be a sham.
The husband did not explain why the name of the borrower is B2 Pty Ltd as opposed to B Pty Ltd.
The wife identifies by way of Exhibit 6 the husband in an affidavit sworn in these proceedings on 23 November 2024 recorded at that time, by way of an IOU dated 1 October 2023, the husband personally had obtained between the years 2020 and 2022, $6,000,000 in Chinese currency (said to be AUD $1,200,000) “conducted for business cash flow purposes”. The IOU is between the husband and Mr C personally and not with any corporation.
The husband did not explain the inconsistencies in each set of loan documents, including the differing periods for which funds were reportedly advanced.
By way of contrast, in Exhibit 1, a letter from the husband’s solicitors to the wife’s solicitors dated 1 April 2025, identifies that the husband is not in possession of any documents relating to this contended advance.
A ledger of B Pty Ltd for the period from 1 July 2017 to 30 June 2022 in Exhibit 1 at page 17 records the receipt of funds from Mr C commencing on 30 November 2019 to 8 July 2020 in the sum of $640,591. The wife again identifies that the husband does not explain the inconsistency in the sets of documents.
The husband said in his oral submissions, absent evidentiary foundation, that the ATO audit caused a change in accounting software for the corporation from MYOB to Xero, and that “some numbers are different... but reflect the same picture”. Again, it is the husband’s obligation to disclose relevant documents and information pursuant to the Act and the Rules. The husband had the capacity to adduce documents to explain these inconsistencies and related matters and did not do so.
The husband did not disclose the relevant information as to the identity of the receiving institution of the funds said to be remitted.
The husband bears the onus to establish the fact and the terms of any contended loan arrangement. On the evidence before me, the husband has not established to the requisite degree that a finding can be made as to B Pty Ltd having a liability of not less than $500,000 to Mr C as at the date of disposal of Motor Vehicle 1 on 15 April 2026.
Accordingly, the findings of fact that the husband sought that Motor Vehicle 1 was trading stock of B Pty Ltd, and that its disposal was a transaction within the ordinary course of business of that corporation, is not supported on consideration of the evidence before me. Nor is the finding that the proceeds of sale had been remitted to Mr C whether by way of payment of a contended liability of the corporation or for any other purpose.

THE LAW

To obtain injunctive relief for the preservation of property, the wife must establish both that there is an arguable case with sufficient likelihood of success to justify the relief sought, and that the balance of convenience favours the grant of the injunction, in that there is a danger or risk of dissipation of, or dealings with, assets which will frustrate any judgment in her favour (Tsiang & Wu and Ors [2019] FamCAFC 128; (2019) FLC 93-911).
The orders made on 28 November 2024 are the starting point. They direct the terms of the preservation of the property of the parties. They have not been varied. In circumstances where the value of the property of the parties and their liabilities is fluid, the wife contending that much of the property of the parties is held by the husband, and the husband contending that the value of the parties’ liabilities is greater than the value of their assets, $500,000 is a significant sum.
Cast in that light, and having regard to the disclosure failures of the husband as identified earlier in these reasons, I find the wife has an arguable case to justify an injunctive order to preserve the $500,000 received on the sale of Motor Vehicle 1. Having regard to the husband’s disclosure failures, and specifically the evidentiary deficiencies identified earlier in these reasons, the wife has established that there is a danger or risk of further dissipation of the parties’ assets which would frustrate any judgment in her favour.
The husband was on notice by way of the correspondence with the wife’s solicitors in February 2026 that the wife sought that Motor Vehicle 1 is not to be disposed of, with his attention directed to the injunctive order. The content of his reply to the wife’s solicitors does him little credit, he implicitly differentiating between his own property and that of the corporation for the purposes of possibly circumventing the injunctive order restricting and prohibiting the parties’ conduct in dealing with their property interests as made on 28 November 2024.
On 10 April 2026, the husband was put on express notice by way of the wife’s interlocutory relief sought in her Amended Initiating Application that she proposed the vehicle be transferred to her and for it to be characterised as an interim property settlement in her favour. Knowing these matters, the husband disposed of the vehicle without notice to the wife.
An injunctive order to give effect to the order proposed by the wife will be made pending further order. The husband has capacity to apply, subject to advice he may or may not receive and as to a risk as to costs, to vary the injunctive order.
In all the circumstances, orders will not be made in the precise terms as sought by the wife but in terms that reflect the implementation of the current circumstances as found in these reasons to achieve her objective.

COSTS

The wife makes an application for costs of and incidental to the determination of her Application in a Proceeding filed on 17 April 2026 and the Response to an Application in a Proceeding of the husband filed on 21 April 2026 on an indemnity basis fixed in the sum of $17,000 or in the alternative on a party/party basis fixed in the sum of $11,000.
The husband opposes any order as to costs. In the event costs are ordered, he seeks they be the subject of agreement or assessment.
Directions will be made for the wife to file material in support of her applications so that the husband has a reasonable opportunity to consider it and put on any material in reply. The wife’s Applications in a Proceeding are listed for hearing before me on 8 May 2026. The costs application will be listed for hearing at the same time.
For all of the above reasons, I make the orders as set out herein.
